This pump fit perfectly, and was far less expensive that the factory OEM part. So far, it's working as expected. It's not a difficult installation process. There are a bunch of videos on YouTube explaining how to access the pump and replace it. Here are a few tips...

1. As others have said, the clamp requires a special type of pliers known as "nippers." I bought a small one at my local tool store for $3.00, and it did the job perfectly. You can replace the clamp with a screw-type clamp, but be aware that for it to fit properly, the width of the clamp needs to be narrower than the usual auto hose clamp. For me, it wasn't worth the hassle of finding a narrow clamp, so I used the supplied clamp with the $3.00 nipper pliers. Worked perfectly.

2. When you install the pump, use a smidge of dish soap on the mounting tang, on the hose fitting, and on the "nose" that goes into the grommet. A bit of dish soap will make everything slide together more easily. Use just a little bit of soap...

3. While you're in there, replace the float micro-switch. It's a cheap part that often fails, and it's easy to access while you're doing the pump replacement. I bought two micro-switches here on Amazon for $15.00. One got installed, the other is a spare.

By doing this job myself, I'm guessing I saved something like $200 as compared to a service call with the OEM part being installed.

I elected to use high quality dimmable LED bulbs instead of the recommended 50W halogen bulbs. Utilitech PAR20 9W (50W equivalent) bulbs work great. There is no flickering and the bulbs are instant on. One review that states one cannot use the lowest light setting without first turning the light on a higher setting. That must be a defect in that user's unit. Mine works perfectly. Several reviewers comment on the heat sensor turning the fan on the high setting when it detects a certain level of heat that the user believes is not present. This could be caused by the use of halogen bulbs which produce a good amount of heat which is another reason to use LED bulbs. Remember the LED bulbs must be dimmable and sometimes lower price/quality bulbs will not work properly. The LED bulbs will produce less heat, last longer and use less electricity than comparable halogen bulbs.

Let me also stress how important it is to get this exact product. There are other Chemex coffee filters that don't seem to do as good of a job as these. The other ones are bleached filters (they look perfectly white, while these look slightly brown), and they don't seem to do as well. I'm rating the product 4 stars (probably premature to do so since it hasn't been installed yet), but based on testing it out and the look of the hood, for the price you can't beat it. My brother dropped ~$2k on a fancy Vent-A-Hood and after seeing this hood he regrets spending so much money.

Update 8/12/2013:
Very impressed with AKDY customer service. They responded within an hour of my e-mail inquiry as to why the hood was advertised as having LED lights, but came with 2x35W Halogens (The halogens my hood came with are 35w, although they are still listed here on Amazon as 30W). I was offered a generous discount to cover the cost of purchasing LED lights; I am happy with the resolution.

Update 12/17/2015:
Up until this week, the hood has been functioning very well. But for the past two nights, the fan has randomly powered on in the middle of the night (unfortunately at around 1AM and 3AM). The electronics seem to have gone haywire. I have sent an e-mail to AKDY to see about resolving the issue. It is within the 3 year warranty, so hopefully they are as good at resolving this issue as they were with the LED light issue. We shall see...
It turned out that the gas sensor had malfunctioned, so the fan was powering on because of the auto-detection of gas feature. I simply removed the sensor and problem solved.

I purchased this pump because my LG French Door refrigerator's water dispenser stopped working. At first the water would trickle out which I assumed was due to a clogged filter. After replacing the filter and still getting low flow, I thought maybe the tubing in the back was clogged so I replaced this as well. When I realized there was nothing left to replace, I searched for the pump. At first I could not seem to find the exact pump. Although entering the part number would generate results, the picture (like the one displayed on this product did not match what I needed.

My original pump had to motors. 1 for the filtered water and 1 for the ice machine. There was only one tube heading out of the pump, up the back of the fridge, and into the filter. Then a tube would come out of the filter make a loop and go back into the fridge thereby supplying filtered water to both the water line and the ice machine.

I ordered this pump as the only option thinking it would not work. Had it not worked, I would have either gone to purchase a new fridge or just stopped using the water and ice dispenser. When it arrived, I realized I had to remove the second pump that it comes with. This was very easily done and reversible if necessary. The plugs fit in perfectly and the water line fit good too.

After several month, the fridge has been working perfectly. Water line pressure is back to normal, ice machine is producing ice again. No leaks. Wish I would have purchased this before all the filter changes and water line retubing. Granted, I refurbished the entire system.

From the looks of this pump, if you have an LG fridge with water and ice maker, this pump should work as it seems it is compatible with multiple different fridges although I am not sure about any other brands. My fridge is a French Door purchased back in 2007. Still works great now that this pump has been replaced.

The red light is extremely accurate. I can tell from the taste when the filter needs changing. I only have two complaints:

(1) They need to include the instructions for turning off the red light indicator on the box or in the directional insert. Press the alarm key for 30 sections and it will turn back to blue.

(2) When I called on 3/24/14 to ask if the product was BPA free, they left me on hold for 8 minutes and then I received a disconnect signal--loud. So, perhaps this answers my question regarding BPA toxic materials. The key questions begs to be asked: How does this affect the healthfulness of this product?

FYI--

BPA stands for bisphenol A. BPA is an industrial chemical that has been used to make certain plastics and resins since the 1960s.

BPA is found in polycarbonate plastics and epoxy resins. Polycarbonate plastics are often used in containers that store food and beverages, such as water bottles. They may also be used in other consumer goods.

Our Whirlpool upright freeze is getting close to 20 years old, and had been making a loud knocking sound off-and-on for some time. I recently shut the freezer down and removed the evaporator shield cover, expecting to see the area covered with ice, including the area of the fan. I thought the fan was hitting ice and making the knocking sound. I was surprised to find that the fan had no ice around it, but it was damaged. The plastic blades had moved up the motor shaft until it was hitting the front of the fan motor, chopping square holes in the leading edge of all four of the fan blades. But as it turned out, this was not the source of the loud knocking! This was a result of what was causing the noise! The problem originated with the fan motor: The front bronze bearing (this bearing "floats" in a small "carrier" inside the bearing housing) had worn enough that it allowed the motor windings (this is a "brush-less" motor) to literally wobble and hit the motor's frame that surround the motor windings. Apparently, all of these collisions between the windings and the frame caused the motor shaft to abruptly seize then begin rotating again, beginning the whole process over and over again. Each stop-then-start sequence apparently caused the plastic fan to "walk" its way up the motor shift until it began hitting the motor's mounting nuts, damaging it. So, in short, our freezer needed an new set of fan blades and a new fan motor. The fan blade and the motor were both FedEx'ed, taking 9 days for the fan blade and 11 days for the motor. With the fan and motor removed, I simply turned the freezer back on and opened the door as little as possible. We lost no food during all of this, but I still wished that shipping could have been faster.  Both items are the real "manufacturer" items, with the exact part numbers and match the original parts perfectly, Our Whirlpool is running silently and perfectly, once again.

This pump fit perfectly in my WF220ANW/XAA Samsung front load washer. It was identical to the oem one it replaced yet much cheaper than elsewhere. The original rusted out and seized from a very slow "leak" from the detergent compartment.

The "leak" was caused by dirty / plugged detergent drawer nozzles and siphon which caused the water being sprayed into it to accumulate in the top tracks of the compartment lid seam. When the washer was rinsing/spinning the vibration would cause that water to drip out from tracks, down to the bottom of the washer where the pump was. Very little water came out, but enough to slowly rust the pump and the bottom of the washer over the years. I only found out about it when the pump died.

I fixed the leak by deep cleaning the detergent tray and compartment. I also reduced the water pressure to prevent it from splashing upward (apparently a known issue).

This filter is very sturdy and well made. The 100 micron size mesh is perfect for making cold brew. The dutch weave is a superior manufacturing technique. The pores allow for good diffusion and easy clean up without residue. Those who are complaining about residue please take note.; a very course grind is recommended for cold brew and will leave little to no residue.

I have made a few batches so far. I did not need to use the gasket as the seal was very tight. I like to make a concentrate using 4:1 to 5:1 water to coffee and then dilute 2:1 for drinking. Using this filter in a 32 oz mason jar I get about 12 to 14 oz of concentrate which will yield about 4-5 8oz servings The coffee is great, I am enjoying a cup as I write this. Since cold brew will last a few weeks in the fridge I would like to make larger batches. I will probable order the larger size filter from Klein and use a 64 oz jar for brewing.

The seller was very helpful and responsive to my questions before I purchased.

Bottom line- I am very happy with this filter and I have no doubt it will hold up for years of use. Highly recommended.

Update: I found the "larger" Klein filter, although longer, has the same capacity due to it being tapered. I would prefer to make more concentrate at 4:1 since the concentrate will keep for 2-3 weeks in the fridge. To do that I would need a filter with a larger capacity. Simply adding more water with the same amount of grounds will obviously make a more dilute brew. I tried it in a 64 Oz jar and the result was not good cold brew but not what I consider "concentrate". I just drink that undiluted. I do not understand how other reviewers can claim they are making a 4:1 concentrate in a 64 Oz jar. That said this is still a very well made product, I just wish they made one with about twice the capacity.

When I received pitcher, it is clearly labelled "48 oz" when Amazon had it advertised as 56 oz., though that advertised size seems to be fixed now.
I am a Barista and make a couple hundred lattes a day at a busy coffee shop on Maui. We ordered this pitcher because we sometimes have to make several large lattes at a time, and also like the spout for latte art. Unfortunately, if the pitcher is more than about 1/2 full, it is impossible to pour without it dribbling down the side of the pitcher. It consistently leaves a small pool of milk next to the cup I am pouring. I have tried a fast pour, slow pour, all different milks, and it is always spilling.
So, I have to say, generally I would not recommend this pitcher. I am still using it because I can steam a lot of milk at once, but the mess it makes is a real hassle. If you only fill it halfway, it seems to work better, but then I already have smaller pitchers that work fine.
Incidentally, if you are looking for latte art pitchers, the smaller Rattleware pitchers have properly designed spouts that do not dribble. If you look closely, the tip of the spout on the smaller ones is slightly lower and angled out on the smaller pitchers. This 48 oz pitcher has the spout exactly parallel to the pitcher's rim. As an earlier reviewer mentions, the spout could use a redesign.
Finally, for latte art, my coworker was quick to point out that the smaller pitchers offer much more control and this huge one is very difficult to get detailed latte art out of.

This has some good points and bad points which, in the end, almost evened out but the short of it is that I will probably stick with the OEM GE filters in the future. Here is the long of it:

Pros:
- inexpensive
- did fit my side by side GE fridge in the place of the GE MSWF filter (a bit tighter to get it to screw on than OEM, but did go on with a good shove and turn)
- once I sorted out the beginning issues, it does give good tasting clean water

Cons:
- Had to run water for 4-5 minutes before all the charcoal dust was out
- Even still, first few glasses of water after that tasted a little off
- During that whole first long water run, it made a helluva racket, I almost took it out for fear of things breaking
- Since then no more loud noises but waterflow is slower than with OEM filter and it makes more noise (I can hear the water running through the filter even with the door closed)

Based on the noises and having to run water through it so much before I could even realistically use it, I doubt the lifespan will be as long as they claim, but I'll keep using it until it dies or leaks or whatever it plans on doing. After that, though, I am going to stick with the OEM GE filters - a lot less hassle and much quicker initial use.

Note: I received this at a discounted rate for inspection, testing, and reviewing purposes. My review is based solely on my personal experience with it.
